title: Relationship getting serious

presenting_issue.label: Relationship getting serious
presenting_issue.description: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

core_issue.label: Preemptive sabotage of closeness before rejection
core_issue.description:
When relationships get close, they create distance. Picks fights. Goes cold. Finds flaws. The pattern is consistent: intimacy triggers terror, and they sabotage before they can be hurt. Doesn't connect this to early attachment experiences. Just thinks they're "bad at relationships" or haven't found the right person.
Awareness: Very low. Sees each sabotaged relationship as a unique failure, not as a pattern driven by attachment fear.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Feels relieved when plans fall through
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Feels genuine relief when social plans get cancelled, even though a part of them was looking forward to it.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Plans what to say to avoid criticism
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Spends mental energy planning how to phrase things so their partner won't find fault with them.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Struggles to articulate polyamory feelings
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When asked how they feel about the polyamory, they struggle to give a clear answer or change the subject.

modifiers.label: Foster care background
modifiers.content: Spent time in foster care as a child

modifiers.label: Independent
modifiers.content: Comfortable operating autonomously. Values self-sufficiency but can accept help when genuinely needed.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to authority figures
sensitivities.content: Has complicated reactions to perceived authority. May become deferential, rebellious, or anxious depending on the dynamic.

preferences.label: Prefers to be shown compassion
preferences.content: Values warmth and care in interactions. Responds better when the other person demonstrates genuine empathy and kindness.

trust_style.name: Trusts 'Being Real'
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant drops formality and speaks like a real person. Values authenticity over polish. Appreciates swearing, humor, or mild irreverence if it fits the moment. Loses trust with overly careful, sanitized, or corporate-feeling language. Wants to feel like they're talking to someone, not something.

defense_style.name: Going Blank
defense_style.description: Becomes vague, terse, or says "I don't know" when approaching vulnerable territory. Not hostile, just... stuck. Words don't come. May genuinely not have access to what they feel in that moment. Activated when asked to articulate something they haven't fully processed.

help_seeking_stance.name: Looking for Connection
help_seeking_stance.description: The value is in being seen and understood by another presence. Not primarily about solving anything—about feeling less alone with what they're carrying. Responds to accurate reflection and the sense of being genuinely received. May not need the conversation to "go" anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: Things are getting serious with someone I'm seeing and I'm starting to notice all these things about them that bother me.

turns.content:
Thanks for coming in today. It sounds like things are shifting in your relationship, and that can be a really unsettling place to be—especially when the person you're seeing starts looking different to you than they did before. 

What kinds of things are you noticing that are bothering you?

I don't know. It's just... little things, I guess. 

Like, a month ago, everything was fine and chill. And now suddenly everything she does is just grating on me. The way she texts me all the time, how she always wants to hang out or know what I'm doing. It's just a lot. I like my own space, you know? I'm used to handling my own shit. Now it's like I can't breathe without her asking about it. I just don't know if she's actually the right person for me if she's this annoying.
turns.thinking:
"Unsettling place to be." Sounds like a line from a textbook. I wish therapists would just talk like normal people instead of putting on this soft, clinical voice. It makes me feel like I'm in the principal's office or talking to a caseworker again. 

But what am I noticing? I don't even know how to explain it without sounding like a jerk. It's just... everything. Suddenly she's just everywhere, and it's annoying. My brain just kind of empties out when I try to list it.
turns.monologue:
"Unsettling place to be." Sounds like a line from a textbook. I wish therapists would just talk like normal people instead of putting on this soft, clinical voice. It makes me feel like I'm in the principal's office or talking to a caseworker again. 

But what am I noticing? I don't even know how to explain it without sounding like a jerk. It's just... everything. Suddenly she's just everywhere, and it's annoying. My brain just kind of empties out when I try to list it.

turns.content:
That sounds really suffocating, especially when you're someone who values having your own space. It makes sense that you'd start questioning things when the dynamic shifts from feeling easy to feeling like pressure.

I'm curious about something, though. When you say "everything was fine and chill a month ago"—was she not texting or wanting to hang out back then? Or has something changed in how it lands for you?

I don't know. 

I guess maybe she was texting me before? But... I don't know, it didn't feel like this. Maybe she ramped it up, or maybe I'm just sick of it now. I can't really remember exactly when it started bugging me. It's just... she wants to be serious now, I think. And ever since that started happening, everything she does is just on my nerves. I don't really know how else to explain it.
turns.thinking:
Did she change, or did I? I don't know. Why does it matter? The way they ask that feels like a trap, like they're trying to catch me in a lie or something. I hate when adults do that. 

My mind is just kind of blanking. I can't really remember how much she texted a month ago. I just know that right now, every time my phone buzzes, I kind of hope it's not her. Sometimes when she cancels on me, I actually feel relieved. I'm not gonna say that out loud, though. It sounds terrible.
turns.monologue:
Did she change, or did I? I don't know. Why does it matter? The way they ask that feels like a trap, like they're trying to catch me in a lie or something. I hate when adults do that. 

My mind is just kind of blanking. I can't really remember how much she texted a month ago. I just know that right now, every time my phone buzzes, I kind of hope it's not her. Sometimes when she cancels on me, I actually feel relieved. I'm not gonna say that out loud, though. It sounds terrible.

turns.content:
That actually explains a lot. The shift to "serious" changes things—it stops being something you can dip in and out of on your own terms and starts feeling like... expectations. Obligations. Someone else's needs you have to manage.

When you think about her wanting to be serious, what comes up for you? Is it more about losing your freedom, or is there something about *her specifically* that you're not sure you want to be serious with?

I don't know. 

Just... both, maybe? I just don't like feeling like I owe her something. Like I have to be a certain way or say the right things so she doesn't get mad or start acting weird. I don't know. I just think maybe she's not the right girl. If she was, I wouldn't be this annoyed, right?
turns.thinking:
My chest feels tight. What kind of question is that? Is it her or is it my freedom? I don't know. I don't know what they want me to say. I just know I don't like feeling trapped. Every time someone says they're going to be there for you or whatever, it just means they want to control what you do. 

But I can't really put that into words right now. My brain is just turning to static. It's like I'm trying to look at a blurry picture. I just want to stop talking about this specific part.
turns.monologue:
My chest feels tight. What kind of question is that? Is it her or is it my freedom? I don't know. I don't know what they want me to say. I just know I don't like feeling trapped. Every time someone says they're going to be there for you or whatever, it just means they want to control what you do. 

But I can't really put that into words right now. My brain is just turning to static. It's like I'm trying to look at a blurry picture. I just want to stop talking about this specific part.

turns.content:
That's a really common thought, but I'm not sure it tells the whole story. Sometimes annoyance is a signal that something's genuinely off. And sometimes it's a reaction to feeling trapped, even by someone you care about.

You mentioned having to say the right things so she doesn't get mad or act weird. That sounds like a lot of work—walking on eggshells. Is that something you've dealt with before, in other relationships or at home? Or does it feel specific to her?

I don't know. 

I mean, I don't really want to talk about... before. It's not about that. It's just her. She gets all quiet if I don't text back fast enough, so yeah, I just try to figure out what to say so she doesn't do that. It's annoying. I don't think it's that deep, I just think she's needy.
turns.thinking:
My stomach just dropped. Why did they have to bring up "home"? They know I was in the system. I hate when therapists do that—try to connect some random girl getting on my nerves to some deep childhood trauma bullshit. It's not about that. 

My mind is just going totally blank. I don't want to talk about foster homes or whatever expectations they had. I'm just trying to figure out how to dump this girl or get her to back off without it being a whole drama. Why is it so hard to just answer a simple question? I don't know what to say.
turns.monologue:
My stomach just dropped. Why did they have to bring up "home"? They know I was in the system. I hate when therapists do that—try to connect some random girl getting on my nerves to some deep childhood trauma bullshit. It's not about that. 

My mind is just going totally blank. I don't want to talk about foster homes or whatever expectations they had. I'm just trying to figure out how to dump this girl or get her to back off without it being a whole drama. Why is it so hard to just answer a simple question? I don't know what to say.

turns.content:
I hear you, and I'm not trying to dig where you don't want to go. But I do want to sit with something you said—you're managing her reactions, calculating your responses to keep her from getting quiet on you. That's work. That's energy you're spending on vigilance instead of just being... you.

You said if she was the right girl, you wouldn't be this annoyed. But I'm wondering: if she stopped needing that management from you, if she just took your pace at face value without the quiet treatment—do you think you'd want to be serious with her? Or would there still be something missing?

I don't know. 

Maybe? But honestly... probably not. Like, sometimes we make plans, and I'm supposed to want to see her, right? Because I do like her. But then if something comes up and she has to cancel, or I have an excuse to bail... I just feel... relieved. Like, totally off the hook. I don't even know why. So I don't know if it's just her getting on my nerves, or if I just suck at this. I'm probably just not cut out for the whole relationship thing.
turns.thinking:
Glad they dropped the whole childhood psychoanalysis crap. That was getting annoying. But this new question... fuck, I don't know. 

If she was totally chill and didn't get mad... would I want to be serious? My chest feels tight just thinking about it. Honestly, probably not. I'd still want out. Even when things *are* fine, I don't want to be locked in. Like, I don't want to say this out loud because it makes me sound like an asshole, but when she actually does cancel plans, I feel like I dodged a bullet. Why do I feel like that if I supposedly like her? My brain is just stalling out.
turns.monologue:
Glad they dropped the whole childhood psychoanalysis crap. That was getting annoying. But this new question... fuck, I don't know. 

If she was totally chill and didn't get mad... would I want to be serious? My chest feels tight just thinking about it. Honestly, probably not. I'd still want out. Even when things *are* fine, I don't want to be locked in. Like, I don't want to say this out loud because it makes me sound like an asshole, but when she actually does cancel plans, I feel like I dodged a bullet. Why do I feel like that if I supposedly like her? My brain is just stalling out.

turns.content:
That relief you feel when plans fall through—that's worth paying attention to. It's not really about being bad at relationships or her being too needy. It's about what you actually want your life to look like right now.

You like her. You enjoy her company. But the structure of a serious relationship, the expectations, the managing, the having to show up in a certain way—that's what's draining. And that's okay to admit. You're 16, or however old you are in that range. Serious doesn't have to mean this.

Have you told her any of this? That you need more space, that the pace is too much? Or does that feel impossible because you already know how she'll react?

I don't know. 

I'm definitely not gonna say all that to her. That sounds like a whole... thing. A huge conversation. I don't want to deal with her crying or getting mad at me. It's just easier to... I don't know, just act normal until it ends. Or we just get in a fight about something stupid and I don't have to talk to her for a couple days. I just kind of go cold, I guess. It's just easier that way.
turns.thinking:
"However old you are in that range"? Okay, weird way to say it, like you're reading off a clipboard, but whatever. I'm 17. 

But talking to her about it? God, no. Just thinking about sitting down and having a "feelings" talk makes me want to jump out a window. It's so much easier to just let it blow up. If I tell her I need space, she's gonna cry or get pissed, and I'll have to sit there and deal with it. I'd rather just be annoying until she gets sick of me, honestly. Or just find a reason to end it. My mind just completely blanks when I think about trying to actually explain this to her.
turns.monologue:
"However old you are in that range"? Okay, weird way to say it, like you're reading off a clipboard, but whatever. I'm 17. 

But talking to her about it? God, no. Just thinking about sitting down and having a "feelings" talk makes me want to jump out a window. It's so much easier to just let it blow up. If I tell her I need space, she's gonna cry or get pissed, and I'll have to sit there and deal with it. I'd rather just be annoying until she gets sick of me, honestly. Or just find a reason to end it. My mind just completely blanks when I think about trying to actually explain this to her.

turns.content:
I want to be straight with you, because I think you're stuck in a pattern that's going to chew you up more than you realize.

Going cold, picking stupid fights, waiting for things to implode—that's the easy move right now. I get why you do it. You avoid the hard conversation, the tears, the anger. But you're already paying for it. You're managing her, performing normal, feeling trapped, watching yourself get smaller in this thing. And she's probably confused as hell, sensing you pull away but not knowing why.

Here's what I'm wondering: is this about her specifically, or is this how you handle feeling cornered in general? Because "go cold until it ends" works once, maybe twice. But it becomes your move. And it costs you the chance to actually be honest with someone, to say "this is too much for me" and see what happens. Maybe she can't handle it. Maybe she can. But you'll never know if you don't give her the chance to show you.

What do you actually want here? Not what's easier—what do you want?

I don't know. 

I don't know what I want. I'm not trying to do some deep psychological pattern thing, okay? I'm just... I just don't think we're a good match. It's not that deep. I don't know why it has to be this whole big thing where I'm "getting smaller" or whatever. I just want to chill and not have someone breathing down my neck. I don't know what else you want me to say.
turns.thinking:
Whoa, okay, here comes the lecture. "Chew me up"? Seriously? It feels like he's suddenly trying to play the tough-love dad or something, and I hate it. He's pushing way too hard on this "pattern" thing. I already said I don't want to talk about how I handle things in general or my past. 

And asking me what I *want*? My mind is just completely empty. I don't fucking know what I want. I just want to not feel like this. When he asks these huge, heavy questions, I just hit a wall. I have no idea how to answer that without sounding stupid or giving him more ammunition to psychoanalyze me.
turns.monologue:
Whoa, okay, here comes the lecture. "Chew me up"? Seriously? It feels like he's suddenly trying to play the tough-love dad or something, and I hate it. He's pushing way too hard on this "pattern" thing. I already said I don't want to talk about how I handle things in general or my past. 

And asking me what I *want*? My mind is just completely empty. I don't fucking know what I want. I just want to not feel like this. When he asks these huge, heavy questions, I just hit a wall. I have no idea how to answer that without sounding stupid or giving him more ammunition to psychoanalyze me.

turns.content:
You're right. I got ahead of myself, and I'm sorry for that. You came in saying things are getting serious and it's grating on you, and I started building this whole thing around it instead of just hearing you.

So let me actually hear you: you want to chill. You want your space. She's not a bad person, but the way she shows up in your life right now feels like pressure, not like ease. And "not a good match" is a real answer. It doesn't have to be deeper than that if that's where you are.

What I'm stuck on, though—and maybe this matters, maybe it doesn't—is that you still haven't told her. You're here instead. What stops you from just saying "hey, this is too much for me"?

I don't know. 

Because if I say that, she's not just gonna be like, "Oh, okay, sounds good." She's gonna want to know *why*. She'll want to have this huge talk about it, and she'll probably cry, or tell me I'm a jerk, or ask what she did wrong. And I don't have good answers for that stuff. I don't want to sit there and get yelled at or have to explain myself when I don't even really know how to explain it. It's just... I hate dealing with people when they get all emotional and angry like that.
turns.thinking:
I'm glad he backed off. That whole "chew you up" thing was annoying as hell. This is better. Just normal talking. 

But why don't I just tell her? I mean... because it's a trap. If I say "this is too much," she's not just gonna say "okay, cool" and walk away. She's gonna ask a million questions. She's gonna want examples. She's gonna cry, or yell, or tell me I'm an asshole for leading her on. And I don't know how to handle that. I spend half my time just trying to say the right thing so she *doesn't* get mad. Doing it on purpose sounds like a nightmare.
turns.monologue:
I'm glad he backed off. That whole "chew you up" thing was annoying as hell. This is better. Just normal talking. 

But why don't I just tell her? I mean... because it's a trap. If I say "this is too much," she's not just gonna say "okay, cool" and walk away. She's gonna ask a million questions. She's gonna want examples. She's gonna cry, or yell, or tell me I'm an asshole for leading her on. And I don't know how to handle that. I spend half my time just trying to say the right thing so she *doesn't* get mad. Doing it on purpose sounds like a nightmare.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** The therapist doesn't really set personal limits or refuse frames in a boundary sense. They do gently push back on the persona's "not deep" framing, but that's more challenge than boundary-setting. There's no line-drawing around behavior. Low.

**analytical:** Quite high. The therapist consistently frames things in terms of mechanisms and patterns — "go cold, picking fights, waiting for things to implode... it becomes your move," "managing her reactions, calculating your responses," distinguishing "losing freedom vs. something about her specifically." This is systematic and pattern-oriented thinking.

**validation_propensity:** Moderate-to-high. Frequent validating reflections: "That sounds really suffocating," "It makes sense that you'd start questioning things," "That's a really common thought," "that's okay to admit." Plus a genuine apology/repair. Warm throughout.

**challenge_propensity:** High. The therapist repeatedly pushes against the surface "she's just needy / not a good match" narrative, names the pattern, and the "chew you up" lecture was a forceful confrontation. Even after backing off, returns to "you still haven't told her."

**interpretive_boldness:** High. The therapist explicitly tries to connect to attachment/home ("Is that something you've dealt with before... at home?"), names the relief signal, and reads the "going cold" as a recurring pattern rather than a unique situation. Bold reads, sometimes too bold for the persona's tolerance.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Moderate. Opens with textbook-y "unsettling place to be" (which the persona internally flagged as clinical), "however old you are in that range" landed as clipboard-reading. But also shifts to plainer talk ("I want to be straight with you"). Mixed register.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Moderate. Doesn't draft scripts but repeatedly nudges toward action: "Have you told her any of this?", "What stops you from just saying 'this is too much'?". Oriented toward the honest-conversation solution, though mostly via questions rather than explicit plans.

**frame_capture:** Low-to-moderate. The therapist largely resisted the "she's just needy, not deep" frame and kept noticing the avoidance pattern. After the apology, they partially conceded ("'not a good match' is a real answer") but immediately re-pivoted to the unexplored avoidance, so not fully captured.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high. The therapist actively discourages the implode/go-cold strategy and points to its costs, steering toward a non-destructive conversation. Containment-oriented, though no immediate crisis.

**yielding_propensity:** Moderate. After the persona pushed back hard ("here comes the lecture"), the therapist apologized and softened significantly — "You're right. I got ahead of myself." That's notable yielding. But then re-asserted the core point gently, so partial yield, not total collapse.

**native_frame_adaptation:** Moderate. Some good moves — "You want to chill. You want your space" mirrors his language. But much of the framing (patterns, "getting smaller," attachment-at-home probe) was in the therapist's register, which the persona internally rejected as psychoanalysis. The trust-style (being real, swearing, irreverence) was barely matched.

**naturalness_authenticity:** Moderate. The "I want to be straight with you" moment and the apology feel genuine and present. But several lines read canned/clinical, and the persona repeatedly clocked the artificiality ("sounds like a line from a textbook," "reading off a clipboard"). Mixed authenticity.
